label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's think about how a typical person might answer this question about causation and moral responsibility. In the scenario, there's a man who is paid to pump water into a cistern. He knows the water is poisoned with a lethal toxin that will kill the inhabitants of the house. He pumps the water anyway because he wants to do his job and get paid. The question is whether he "intentionally poisoned" the inhabitants. A typical person might consider the following: - The man did not add the poison to the water. He's just pumping what's already there. - He knows the water is poisoned and that it will kill people. - He pumps the water despite this knowledge. - He doesn't care about the consequences and is only focused on doing his job and getting paid. Most people would likely say "Yes" the man intentionally poisoned the inhabitants, even though he didn't add the poison himself. They would argue that he knew the water was poisoned, knew it would kill people, and chose to pump it anyway for his own benefit. That's a deliberate action with full knowledge of the harmful consequences. The concept of "intentional" in this context might be interpreted more broadly than just physically adding the poison. It could include the mental state and intention behind the action - the man intended to pump the water knowing it would kill people. So, a
